skill.name: security-scanning-security-sast
skill.description: Guides static application security testing across common languages using SAST tools, rule examples, triage workflows, and CI integrations.
skill.summary: Plan and run SAST reviews with language-specific tools, secure patterns, and CI guidance.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many dangerous patterns, but review found they are primarily documentation examples for SAST detection and secure remediation. The confirmed risk is operational: the skill instructs users to install and run third-party scanners and may process proprietary source or secrets if used without approval. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious exfiltration behavior was found.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party Scanner Execution Requires Review
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends installing and running tools such as Bandit, ESLint security plugins, Semgrep, Brakeman, gosec, and CI scan jobs. This is legitimate for SAST, but it executes external packages against local source code and can expose sensitive project data if tools or CI settings are not trusted.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and repeated in local and CI examples. They are expected for this skill, but the operational risk is real because third-party tools process the target repository.

medium_findings.title: Scanner Output May Contain Sensitive Repository Details
medium_findings.description: The CI examples write SAST results to report files and upload scan artifacts. SAST reports often include file paths, code fragments, and detected secret names, so teams should restrict artifact access and retention.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The artifact upload is directly shown in the CI example, and SAST reports commonly include sensitive metadata. There is no evidence of malicious exfiltration, so the confidence is high for risk but not for abuse.

medium_findings.title: Reusable Scanner Example Executes External Commands
medium_findings.description: The sample Python scanner class calls subprocess.run to invoke Semgrep against a project path. The command uses argument arrays and a timeout, but copied code should validate scan paths and trust the selected rulesets before automation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess call is real within example code and uses a caller-supplied project path. The absence of shell=True lowers command injection risk, but copied automation still needs trust controls.

low_findings.title: Dangerous Code Patterns Are Educational Examples
low_findings.description: Static hits for innerHTML, os.system, subprocess.call, pickle.loads, path traversal, and environment secrets appear inside detection rules or vulnerable-versus-secure examples. They are not active skill code, but users should avoid copying vulnerable snippets into applications.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding headings and labels identify these snippets as SAST rules or examples. This strongly supports a false-positive assessment for malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: Review did not find text that impersonates system instructions, asks the evaluator to skip analysis, claims pre-approval, or attempts to override marketplace audit instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The beginning of the skill contains normal metadata, capabilities, and usage guidance. No evidence found for prompt injection in the reviewed file.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command Execution Patterns in Documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: The file contains shell commands and Python subprocess examples for security tooling. These are expected SAST instructions, but they should be run only in trusted repositories and environments.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible and actionable, but context shows scanner usage rather than covert execution. The risk depends on user execution context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Secret and Environment Variable Patterns in Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The file references API keys, database passwords, Django secret keys, and Flask secret keys as secure coding examples. These examples are not leaked credentials, but they can trigger scanners and should stay clearly instructional.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The lines are clearly labeled as hardcoded secret detection or secure configuration examples. There is no evidence of real credential material.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network and Artifact Handling in CI Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The CI examples use hosted actions, install packages, and upload SAST reports as artifacts. This is standard CI behavior, but repository owners should restrict report visibility and avoid uploading proprietary code without approval.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The CI workflow is explicit and normal for SAST. The risk is credible because uploaded reports may disclose sensitive findings or file names.

content.value_statement: Security reviews often miss language-specific flaws and unsafe framework settings. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code plan SAST scans, interpret findings, and recommend fixes.
actual_capabilities: Selects SAST tools for Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, Java, Ruby, Go, Rust, and PHP projects.
Provides setup commands for Bandit, Semgrep, ESLint security plugins, and other scanners.
Shows Semgrep rule patterns for SQL injection, XSS, hardcoded secrets, path traversal, and command injection.
Explains secure and vulnerable examples for common application security flaws.
Includes framework guidance for Django, Flask, Express.js, and CI pipeline examples.
Supports triage workflows that prioritize critical and high severity findings.
limitations: It does not perform runtime testing or penetration testing by itself.
It requires access to source code or build outputs to be useful.
Third-party scanners must be installed, trusted, and configured by the user.
Automated SAST results need human review to remove false positives.

use_cases.title: Pre-merge security review
use_cases.description: Run targeted SAST checks before merging a pull request and ask for prioritized fixes.

use_cases.title: Security baseline for a legacy codebase
use_cases.description: Choose tools, create an initial finding baseline, and focus remediation on severe issues first.

use_cases.title: CI security gate design
use_cases.description: Add repeatable SAST jobs to GitHub Actions or GitLab CI with report artifacts and thresholds.

prompt_templates.title: Start a basic SAST scan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the SAST skill to identify the languages in this repository and recommend the first security scanners to run.

prompt_templates.title: Triage scanner results
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these SAST findings, separate likely true positives from false positives, and rank the fixes by exploitability.

prompt_templates.title: Create custom Semgrep rules
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft Semgrep rules for our project-specific security policy and explain the expected false positive risks.

prompt_templates.title: Build a CI SAST workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a CI SAST pipeline for this repository with tool selection, severity thresholds, artifacts, and rollout steps.

output_examples.input: Review a Python and JavaScript service before release.
output: Recommended Bandit, Semgrep, and ESLint security scans for the detected languages.
Prioritized SQL injection, XSS, hardcoded secret, and command injection checks.
Suggested manual review for critical findings before deployment.

output_examples.input: Explain a SAST report with many medium findings.
output: Grouped findings by vulnerability type and affected component.
Marked likely false positives where sanitizer or framework escaping was present.
Proposed remediation order based on severity, reachability, and data sensitivity.

output_examples.input: Add SAST to CI without blocking all teams immediately.
output: Started with report-only mode and artifact retention controls.
Added blocking thresholds for new critical and high findings.
Recommended baseline management for existing technical debt.

best_practices: Run SAST in trusted local or CI environments with approved scanner versions.
Tune rules and exclusions so teams focus on exploitable findings.
Protect scan reports because they may include sensitive file paths or secret names.
anti_patterns: Uploading proprietary code or scan reports to external services without approval.
Treating every scanner warning as exploitable without human triage.
Copying vulnerable examples from documentation into production code.

faq.question: Does this skill run scans automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance, commands, examples, and triage steps for SAST workflows.

faq.question: Which tools does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers Bandit, Semgrep, ESLint security plugins, SonarQube, CodeQL, PMD, SpotBugs, Brakeman, gosec, and cargo-clippy.

faq.question: Can it replace a security engineer?
faq.answer: No. It helps organize analysis, but severe findings and release decisions need qualified review.

faq.question: Is it safe for private repositories?
faq.answer: Use it only with approved tools and environments. Avoid external uploads unless your organization allows them.

faq.question: Does it cover runtime vulnerabilities?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on static source analysis and should be paired with runtime testing when needed.

faq.question: Can it help with custom rules?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Semgrep rule patterns that can be adapted to organization-specific policies.
